planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or hardwood cuttings in fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; sweet mock orange blooms on the previous year's wood — prune immediately after flowering by removing the oldest, flowered stems to encourage new growth from the base.

directions: Root cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; sweet mock orange is a European deciduous shrub that produces one of the most intensely and purely sweet fragrances of any flowering shrub available to temperate gardeners — the large, pure-white, four-petaled flowers, borne in dense clusters along the arching branches in late May to June, fill the garden air with a sweet orange-blossom and honey fragrance that is produced in such quantity that a single established shrub can perfume an entire garden. The flowers have a simple, classic, white-on-white beauty that complements the fragrance perfectly. Individual flowers are relatively brief (2–3 weeks), but the overall display is impressive and the fragrance lingers. 'Aureus' has golden-yellow spring foliage that fades to chartreuse-green in summer; 'Variegatus' has white-margined leaves. It tolerates partial shade and clay soil better than most fragrant shrubs. Prune immediately after flowering to maintain shape and encourage vigorous flowering growth for the following year. One of the most valuable and time-tested fragrant flowering shrubs in the temperate garden tradition.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or hardwood cuttings in winter, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; weigela blooms on old wood — prune immediately after flowering by removing the oldest, flowered stems to prevent the shrub from becoming overgrown and bare at the base.

directions: Root cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; weigela is a Chinese deciduous flowering shrub that has been a cornerstone of the North American spring garden since the Victorian era — producing masses of trumpet-shaped, 1-inch flowers densely covering the arching branches in late May to June in shades ranging from deep crimson-red and rose-pink to pale pink and white, depending on cultivar, at the same time as azaleas and late lilacs, creating one of the richest spring flowering displays available in zone 4–8. Modern cultivars have dramatically expanded the ornamental value beyond the original species, adding dark-purple, burgundy, or gold foliage to the flowering display: 'Wine and Roses' (deep-purple foliage, bright-pink flowers), 'Midnight Wine' (compact, dwarf, burgundy leaves, rose-pink flowers), and 'Sonic Bloom' series (repeat-flowering from spring through frost) are standout introductions. It attracts hummingbirds reliably. Tolerates partial shade acceptably. One of the easiest and most adaptable flowering shrubs for the temperate garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or hardwood cuttings in late fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; deutzia blooms on old wood and should be pruned immediately after flowering by thinning the oldest, flowered stems to the ground to maintain vigor and density.

directions: Root cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade; fuzzy deutzia is a Chinese and Japanese deciduous shrub that creates a spectacular late-spring and early-summer flowering display — the upright to arching branches become completely covered from tip to base with dense, upright panicles of white or pink-tinged, five-petaled flowers in late May to June, creating one of the fullest and most exuberant white-flowered shrub displays of the season. The name 'scabra' (rough) refers to the distinctive, rough, scurfy, sandpaper-textured surface of the ovate leaves, which distinguish it from the smooth-leaved species. 'Plena' with double, rose-flushed flowers, 'Pride of Rochester' with large, semi-double, white flowers with pink reverses, and 'Nikko' (dwarf, ground-cover habit) are the most widely planted cultivars. Tolerates partial shade acceptably. After a few years, the arching stems develop attractive, warm-brown, exfoliating bark that provides additional winter interest. An easy, trouble-free, and long-lived deciduous shrub that has been reliably ornamental in temperate gardens for well over a century.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in midsummer under mist, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Hartlage Wine is a sterile hybrid and must be propagated vegetatively from cuttings — it does not set seed.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in moist, average to humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Hartlage Wine sweetshrub is a sterile hybrid (Calycanthus floridus × C. chinensis) developed at JC Raulston Arboretum in Raleigh, North Carolina, that combines the best qualities of both parents into a superior, vigorous, large deciduous shrub with remarkable ornamental value — the outstanding flowers, produced in extraordinary abundance from May through August on a long season matched by few large shrubs, are large (2–3 inches across), multi-petaled, deep wine-red to burgundy, and produced on both old and new wood, maintaining color from late spring through summer. The large, broad, glossy leaves are more refined than those of the native sweetshrub. Unlike the common sweetshrub (Calycanthus floridus), Hartlage Wine flowers reliably in partial shade and does not depend on heat or sun for good flower production. It is sterile and non-invasive. One of the finest and most adaptable flowering shrubs introduced in the late 20th century for zone 6–9 gardens, equally at home in sun or partial shade, and exceptionally free-flowering over its long bloom season.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or layer branches in spring, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; beauty bush is drought-tolerant once established and flowers most freely in full sun — excessive fertility and moisture produce rank growth and reduce flowering.

directions: Root softwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; beauty bush is a Chinese monotypic deciduous shrub — the single species in its genus, and one of the last of the great early 20th-century introductions from E.H. Wilson's plant hunting expeditions in western China — that produces a brief but spectacular flowering display in late May to June when the entire large, arching, vase-shaped shrub becomes enveloped from base to tip in masses of small, pale-pink to rose-pink, yellow-throated, tubular flowers in dense clusters, creating a cloud-like, pale-pink effect that gives the plant its perfectly apt common name. After the flowers fall, the bristly, pink seed heads are themselves attractive for weeks. The shrub has a graceful, arching, fountain-like form with peeling, tan-brown bark that provides winter interest. It tolerates a wide range of soils including alkaline and dry conditions. After the spring flowering peak it is relatively plain but does not detract from the garden. 'Pink Cloud' is an improved selection with denser, deeper-pink flower clusters.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
